title: Chapter 48: Breakthrough
content: The Signal Key hummed against Wren's palm, warm as a living thing.

She pressed it into the terminal port. The server room lights flickered — once, twice — and the screens around them erupted into cascading code. Firewalls. Layers of them, nested deep, built to keep exactly this from happening.

Ash moved beside her. He placed his hand over hers on the Key, and the glitch surged between them like a current completing a circuit. The sensation jolted through her arm, her chest, her spine — electric and vast, as if they'd tapped into something far larger than a network.
"On my mark," she said. Her voice didn't sound like her own. It sounded like a signal.

Vance was shouting somewhere behind them. Security boots on the floor, the clatter of weapons being drawn. The Hounds in the corners of the room had stopped their slow patrol, heads tilting, receiving conflicting commands through their corrupted feeds.
"Now," Wren said.
She pushed. Ash pushed with her.
The first firewall shattered like glass.

The second folded in on itself, code unraveling in long silken threads across the monitors. The third — the one Vance had personally designed, the one that had buried subjects in data graves for years — it resisted. It pushed back. Wren felt it like a hand against her skull, pressure building behind her temples.

Ash gripped her hand tighter. His tattoos were blazing now, lines of white fire running up his forearms, and the glitch amplified between them into something neither had ever produced alone. A resonance. A frequency that cut through every barrier NeuroScale had built.
The third firewall collapsed.
The screens went white. Then black. Then they began to populate — one by one, file after file, scrolling out into the open network. Into the city. Into the world.
SUBJECT 03 — ALEXANDER CHEN — ERASED.

The words appeared on every monitor in the server room. On every phone connected to the Blackmoss internal network. And then — beyond. Leaping the gap, riding the signal outward, reaching every screen, every feed, every device in range.
SUBJECT 07 — PETRA KOVAČ — TERMINATED.

Wren watched the names appear and felt something crack open inside her chest. Not breaking — releasing. Three years of silence, three years of being a footnote in someone else's experiment, pouring out into the open air.
SUBJECT 11 — DANIEL OKAFOR — ERASED.
SUBJECT 14 — LINA SAITO — TERMINATED.
One by one. Every subject. Every name. Every status.

Vance had gone rigid by the security station. Her face was drained of color, lips parted, staring at the screens as her life's buried work unspooled for the world to see. She reached for a keyboard — tried to issue a kill command — and the system rejected her. Access denied. The Signal Key had rewritten her permissions in the space of a heartbeat.
"Shut it down!" she screamed. But no one moved. The security officers were staring at the screens, reading the files, seeing for the first time what they'd been guarding.

The Hounds received the corrupted commands and stood down. Not by choice — by overload. Their targeting protocols couldn't parse the flood of conflicting data. They stood motionless in the corners, hands slack at their sides, processing, recalibrating, getting nothing useful back.

The server room was chaos. Alarms shrieking. Red light washing over everything in pulsing waves. Vance screaming orders no one followed. Security officers stepping back from their stations, reading subject files on their personal phones because the broadcast had already jumped the internal network and was streaming to everything with a signal.
Ash pulled Wren's hand from the terminal. "We need to go."

She didn't want to move. She wanted to stand there and watch every name appear, every life acknowledged, every erasure documented in public record. She wanted to stand there until the last file transmitted and the silence she'd lived inside for three years was finally, irrevocably broken.
But Ash was right. The Hounds would reset. Security would regroup. Vance would find another way.
She grabbed the Signal Key from the port. It came free with a sound like a breath released — a long, low sigh of data finishing its sprint. She shoved it into her jacket and ran.

They dodged between server racks, the metal aisles cold against Wren's shoulders as she squeezed through gaps too narrow for the pursuing officers. The alarms covered the sound of their footsteps, red light and klaxon filling every space, and Ash led them toward the maintenance corridor he'd mapped during his time inside Blackmoss.
A security officer appeared at the end of the row. Wren flickered — just for a moment, just enough — and the officer's gaze passed over her like she was a smudge on the air. She kept moving.

The maintenance door was heavy, sealed with a keypad. Ash typed a code — one he'd stolen from a guard's thoughts weeks ago — and it clicked open. They slipped through into a concrete stairwell, fluorescent lights buzzing overhead, the smell of antiseptic and ozone clinging to the walls.

Down. Three flights. Wren's thighs burned, her lungs tight, the Signal Key a warm weight against her sternum. Behind them, the alarms continued. Above them, floors full of people were reading the names of the dead and the erased.
She didn't look back.

The ground floor exit opened onto a service alley. Rain hit her face — cold, sharp, real. The night air was thick with the smell of wet concrete and diesel, and somewhere in the distance, she could hear the thump of helicopter blades. News choppers. Already circling.
She and Ash pressed against the alley wall, catching their breath. Through a window three stories up, they could see the glow of screens still scrolling data. Still broadcasting.
SUBJECT 22 — AMARA DIOP — ERASED.
SUBJECT 28 — IVAN PETROV — TERMINATED.
The list was still growing. Names she'd never known. Lives she'd never witnessed. People who had been made into nothing, now made into evidence.
Ash's hand found hers in the dark. His fingers were trembling. She held on.

The broadcast was running. Nothing could stop it now — not Vance, not NeuroScale, not the entire apparatus that had built itself on the silence of the unseen. The signal was out. The world was watching.
Every phone in the city was lighting up with the truth.
And Wren was still invisible. But for the first time in three years, she didn't feel erased.
She felt like proof.
